Imputing missing data on the Ecological parameters data set

Floret Justine

2024-10-11

Due to environmental limitations, some ecological parameters were not measured at several sites. These include temperature and humidity measurements using Ibutton, as well as soil collection at sites with limited soil volume, such as walls and pavements (Table 1). Removing the missing data from the data set would result in the loss of other parameters measured at the sites. In order to analyze all the ecological data at every site, we tested two imputation methods in R (version 4.4.1): imputePCA from the package missMDA (version 1.19) and missForest from the package missForest (version 1.5).

The first method, with the missMDA package, is based on a regularized iterative PCA algorithm. It is imputing the missing data first with the mean of each variable, then with PCA to iterate until convergence, avoiding over fitting issues (J. Josse, F. Husson). The second method is based on a random forest algorithm, training it on observed data to impute the missing ones (Stekhoven, Bühlmann).

To test which method infers better the missing values, a sub data set with no missing data was used to randomly and non randomly replace observed values by NA. Both inferring methods were used to impute the missing data on it. Then the imputed data sets were compared to the observed one with Pearson Chi square test, mean squared error, a F test and correlation coefficient.

library(readr)
library("plyr") # Needed for Ellenberg value 
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(reshape2) # melt function
library(forcats) # reorganize in geom boxplot
library(data.table)
library(wakefield) # randomly replace value by na
library(stringr) #  remove strings in a column
library(lmerTest) #glht
library(lmerTest) #glht
library(lsmeans) #pairwise comparison
library(emmeans)
library(multcomp)  #glht
library(multcompView)  #glht
library(rstatix) # manova levene test
library(nnet) # glm with factor
library(GGally) #ppair plot
library(ggplot2)
library(viridis)
library(svglite) # save in svg format
library(shiny)
library(FactoInvestigate)
library(FactoMineR)
library(Factoshiny)
library(missMDA)
library(factoextra)
library("corrplot")
library("Hmisc")
library(missForest)# impute missing value 
library(bigstep)
library(randomForest)
colors <- c("#6a176e", "#bc3754", "#f37819", "#f8cd37")

Preparing the data set.

Ecological_parameters <- read_csv("/home/justine/sciebo/2_Ecological_parameters/2_assays/Ecological_parameters_0.csv")
Ecological_parameters$Site_ID <- as.factor(Ecological_parameters$Site_ID)
Ecological_parameters$Area <- as.factor(Ecological_parameters$Area)
Ecological_parameters$Localization_ID <- as.factor(Ecological_parameters$Localization_ID)
Ecological_parameters$Type <- as.factor(Ecological_parameters$Type)
Ecological_parameters$Destroyed <- as.factor(Ecological_parameters$Destroyed)
Ecological_parameters$Outliers_soil <- as.factor(Ecological_parameters$Outliers_soil)
Ecological_parameters$Sowing_1st <- as.factor(Ecological_parameters$Sowing_1st)
Ecological_parameters$Sowing_2nd <- as.factor(Ecological_parameters$Sowing_2nd)

The sites destroyed before the measurement of the ecological parameters, in April 2022, are removed as well as the site definitively destroyed in April 2023. The measurements were done in the site sown in September 2021, the others sites are also removed from the analysis.

complete_data <- Ecological_parameters %>% filter(Sowing_1st =="09/2021" ) %>% filter(Sowing_2nd =="no")
complete_data <- complete_data %>% filter(Destroyed !="02/2022" ) %>% filter(Destroyed !="04/2023")
complete_data <- complete_data[,c(4,22:151)]
complete_data$Type <- as.factor(complete_data$Type )

I. PCA imputation missing data

This method is based on a regularized iterative PCA algorithm. It is imputing the missing data first with the mean of each variable, then with PCA to iterate until convergence, avoiding over fitting issues (J. Josse, et al, 2010).

First create a data set with no missing data.

data_noNA <- na.omit(complete_data)

1. PCA imputing random NA values

Replace randomly values of the data set with no missing data by NA.

data_randomNA <- data_noNA %>% r_na(cols = 2:131, prob = 0.08)

Then impute the NA with missMDA package.

nPCs <- estim_ncpPCA(data_randomNA[,2:131])
data_randomNA_impute_PCA <- missMDA::imputePCA(data_randomNA, quanti.sup=c(130),quali.sup=c(1), ncp=nPCs$ncp)$completeObs

Compare the imputed data with the observed ones from the data set with no missing value.

Different statistical test are realized to compare the data set with no missing data (observed data) with the data set with imputed data, to infer if the method is imputing correctly.

# Get the common column names
common_columns <- intersect(names(data_noNA[,2:131]), names(data_randomNA_impute_PCA[,2:131]))

# Initialize lists to store correlations and p-values
correlation_results <- list()
pvalue_results <- list()
  • Correlation:
    Looking at the correlation coefficient between the observed datas and the imputed ones.
for (col in common_columns) {
  result <- rcorr(as.matrix(data_noNA[[col]]), as.matrix(data_randomNA_impute_PCA[[col]]))
  correlation_results[[col]] <- result$r[1, 2]  # Extract the correlation coefficient
  pvalue_results[[col]] <- result$P[1, 2]      # Extract the p-value
}

Convert lists to named vectors for easier readability.

correlations <- unlist(correlation_results)
pvalues <- unlist(pvalue_results)
comparison_obs_imputed <- data.frame("Correlations_imputePCA" = correlations, "pvalues_imputePCA" = pvalues)
  • Chi square test:
    Pearson’s Chi-squared Test is testing if there is a significant association between the categories of the two variables.
    H0: the row and the column variables of the contingency table are independent.
    H1: row and column variables are dependent, the observed value and the imputed ones are dependent.
comparison_obs_imputed$Chitest_imputePCA <- rep(99, 130)
for (col in common_columns) {
  results_chitest <- chisq.test(data_noNA[[col]], data_randomNA_impute_PCA[[col]], correct = FALSE)
  comparison_obs_imputed[col,3]  <- results_chitest[["p.value"]]
}
  • Mean squared error:
    MSE is predicting accuracy of a model. It is calculated as: MSE = (1/n) * Σ(actual – prediction)²
    The lower the value for MSE, the more accurately a model is able to predict values.
comparison_obs_imputed$MSE_imputePCA <- rep(99, 130)
for (col in common_columns) {
  comparison_obs_imputed[col,4] <- mean((data_noNA[[col]] - data_randomNA_impute_PCA[[col]])^2)
}
  • F test:
    F-test is used to assess whether the variances of two populations (A and B) are equal, here the observed data and the imputed ones.
    H0: σ1² = σ2² (the population variances are equal).
    /! sensitive to the normality of the sample distribution.
comparison_obs_imputed$Ftest_imputePCA <- rep(99, 130)
for (col in common_columns) {
  F1<- var.test(data_noNA[[col]], data_randomNA_impute_PCA[[col]])
  comparison_obs_imputed[col,5] <- F1[["p.value"]]
}

2. PCA imputing nonrandom NA values

Replace non randomly values of the data set with no missing data by NA.

data_NorandomNA <- data_noNA

# Add NA in Temperature (mean, min, max, DD) Humidity variables
data_NorandomNA <- data_NorandomNA %>%
 mutate(across(3:67, ~ if_else(Type == "pavement", na_if(., .), .)))

# Add NA in N%, C% and Ions in random rows
data_NorandomNA <- data_NorandomNA %>%
 mutate(across(90:96, ~ if_else(row_number() %in% 44:77, na_if(., .), .)))

# Add NA in soil parameters for tree bed
data_NorandomNA <- data_NorandomNA %>%
 mutate(across(69:84, ~ if_else(Type == "Tree bed", na_if(., .), .)))

# Add a little bit of NA randomly in every column
data_NorandomNA <- data_NorandomNA %>% r_na(cols = 2:131, prob = 0.04)

Then impute the NA with missMDA package.

data_NorandomNA_impute_PCA <- missMDA::imputePCA(data_NorandomNA, quanti.sup=c(130),quali.sup=c(1))$completeObs

Compare the imputed data with the observed ones from the data set with no missing value.

  • Correlation:
    Looking at the correlation coefficient between the observed datas and the imputed ones.
correlation_results <- list()
pvalue_results <- list()

# Compute the correlation and p-values for each pair of columns with the same name using rcorr
for (col in common_columns) {
  result <- rcorr(as.matrix(data_noNA[[col]]), as.matrix(data_NorandomNA_impute_PCA[[col]]))
  correlation_results[[col]] <- result$r[1, 2]  # Extract the correlation coefficient
  pvalue_results[[col]] <- result$P[1, 2]      # Extract the p-value
}

Convert lists to named vectors for easier readability

correlations <- unlist(correlation_results)
pvalues <- unlist(pvalue_results)
comparison_obs_NorandomNA <- data.frame("Correlations_imputePCA" = correlations, "pvalues_imputePCA" = pvalues)
  • Chi square test:
    Pearson’s Chi-squared Test is testing if there is a significant association between the categories of the two variables.
    H0: the row and the column variables of the contingency table are independent.
    H1: row and column variables are dependent, the observed value and the imputed ones are dependent.
comparison_obs_NorandomNA$Chitest_imputePCA <- rep(99, 130)
for (col in common_columns) {
  results_chitest <- chisq.test(data_noNA[[col]], data_NorandomNA_impute_PCA[[col]], correct = FALSE)
  comparison_obs_NorandomNA[col,3]  <- results_chitest[["p.value"]]
}
  • Mean squared error
    MSE is predicting accuracy of a model. It is calculated as: MSE = (1/n) * Σ(actual – prediction)²
    The lower the value for MSE, the more accurately a model is able to predict values.
comparison_obs_NorandomNA$MSE_imputePCA <- rep(99, 130)
for (col in common_columns) {
  comparison_obs_NorandomNA[col,4] <- mean((data_noNA[[col]] - data_NorandomNA_impute_PCA[[col]])^2)
}
  • F test
    F-test is used to assess whether the variances of two populations (A and B) are equal, here the observed data and the imputed ones.
    H0: σ1² = σ2² (the population variances are equal).
    /! sensitive to the normality of the sample distribution.
comparison_obs_NorandomNA$Ftest_imputePCA <- rep(99, 130)
for (col in common_columns) {
  F1<- var.test(data_noNA[[col]], data_NorandomNA_impute_PCA[[col]])
  comparison_obs_NorandomNA[col,5] <- F1[["p.value"]]
}

II. Random Forest imputation missing data

This method is based on a random forest algorithm, training it on observed data to impute the missing ones (Stekhoven and Bühlmann,2011).
Information about the random forest package to impute data:
- Petrazzini et al, 2021
- Using the missForest package

1. missForest test imputing ramdom NA values

Use the same data (data_randomNA) than for the PCA method

data_randomNA_impute_MF <- missForest(data_randomNA[,2:131])$ximp #remove factor column
data_randomNA_impute_MF$Type <- data_randomNA$Type

Compare the imputed data with the observed ones from the data set with no missing value.

  • Correlation:
    Looking at the correlation coefficient between the observed datas and the imputed ones.
for (col in common_columns) {
  result <- rcorr(as.matrix(data_noNA[[col]]), as.matrix(data_randomNA_impute_MF[[col]]))
  correlation_results[[col]] <- result$r[1, 2]  # Extract the correlation coefficient
  pvalue_results[[col]] <- result$P[1, 2]      # Extract the p-value
}
correlations <- unlist(correlation_results)
pvalues <- unlist(pvalue_results)
comparison_obs_imputed$Correlations_imputeMF <- as.numeric(correlations)
comparison_obs_imputed$pvalues_imputeMF <- as.numeric(pvalues)
  • Chi square test:
    Pearson’s Chi-squared Test is testing if there is a significant association between the categories of the two variables.
    H0: the row and the column variables of the contingency table are independent.
    H1: row and column variables are dependent, the observed value and the imputed ones are dependent.
comparison_obs_imputed$Chitest_imputeMF <- rep(99, 130)
for (col in common_columns) {
  results_chitest <- chisq.test(data_noNA[[col]], data_randomNA_impute_MF[[col]], correct = FALSE)
  comparison_obs_imputed[col,8]  <- results_chitest[["p.value"]]}
  • Mean squared error MSE is predicting accuracy of a model. It is calculated as: MSE = (1/n) * Σ(actual – prediction)²
    The lower the value for MSE, the more accurately a model is able to predict values.
comparison_obs_imputed$MSE_imputeMF <- rep(99, 130)
for (col in common_columns) {
  comparison_obs_imputed[col,9] <- mean((data_noNA[[col]] - data_randomNA_impute_MF[[col]])^2)
}
  • F test
    F-test is used to assess whether the variances of two populations (A and B) are equal, here the observed data and the imputed ones.
    H0: σ1² = σ2² (the population variances are equal).
    /! sensitive to the normality of the sample distribution.
comparison_obs_imputed$Ftest_imputeMF <- rep(99, 130)
for (col in common_columns) {
  F1<- var.test(data_noNA[[col]], data_randomNA_impute_MF[[col]])
  comparison_obs_imputed[col,10] <- F1[["p.value"]]
}

Transform the table in data frame.

comparison_obs_imputed <- tibble::rownames_to_column(comparison_obs_imputed, "Parameters")

2. missForest imputing nonrandom NA values

Use same data (data_NorandomNA) than for the PCA method

data_NorandomNA_impute_MF <- missForest(data_NorandomNA[,2:131])$ximp 
data_NorandomNA_impute_MF$Type <- data_NorandomNA$Type

Compare the imputed data with the observed ones from the data set with no missing value.

  • Correlation:
    Looking at the correlation coefficient between the observed datas and the imputed ones.
for (col in common_columns) {
  result <- rcorr(as.matrix(data_noNA[[col]]), as.matrix(data_NorandomNA_impute_MF[[col]]))
  correlation_results[[col]] <- result$r[1, 2]  # Extract the correlation coefficient
  pvalue_results[[col]] <- result$P[1, 2]      # Extract the p-value
}
correlations <- unlist(correlation_results)
pvalues <- unlist(pvalue_results)
comparison_obs_NorandomNA$Correlations_imputeMF <- as.numeric(correlations)
comparison_obs_NorandomNA$pvalues_imputeMF <- as.numeric(pvalues)
  • Chi square test:
    Pearson’s Chi-squared Test is testing if there is a significant association between the categories of the two variables.
    H0: the row and the column variables of the contingency table are independent.
    H1: row and column variables are dependent, the observed value and the imputed ones are dependent.
comparison_obs_NorandomNA$Chitest_imputeMF <- rep(99, 130)
for (col in common_columns) {
  results_chitest <- chisq.test(data_noNA[[col]], data_NorandomNA_impute_MF[[col]], correct = FALSE)
  comparison_obs_NorandomNA[col,8]  <- results_chitest[["p.value"]]}
  • Mean squared error
    MSE is predicting accuracy of a model. It is calculated as: MSE = (1/n) * Σ(actual – prediction)²
    The lower the value for MSE, the more accurately a model is able to predict values.
comparison_obs_NorandomNA$MSE_imputeMF <- rep(99, 130)
for (col in common_columns) {
  comparison_obs_NorandomNA[col,9] <- mean((data_noNA[[col]] - data_NorandomNA_impute_MF[[col]])^2)
}
  • F test
    F-test is used to assess whether the variances of two populations (A and B) are equal, here the observed data and the imputed ones.
    H0: σ1² = σ2² (the population variances are equal).
    /! sensitive to the normality of the sample distribution.
comparison_obs_NorandomNA$Ftest_imputeMF <- rep(99, 130)
for (col in common_columns) {
  F1<- var.test(data_noNA[[col]], data_NorandomNA_impute_MF[[col]])
  comparison_obs_NorandomNA[col,10] <- F1[["p.value"]]
}

Transform the table in data frame.

comparison_obs_NorandomNA <- tibble::rownames_to_column(comparison_obs_NorandomNA, "Parameters")

III. Comparison of the PCA and random forest method to impute the missing values

1. Imputation of random NA

Correlation

correlation <- comparison_obs_imputed %>% 
  rowwise() %>% 
  mutate( mymean = mean(c(Correlations_imputePCA,Correlations_imputeMF) )) %>% 
  arrange(mymean) %>% 
  mutate(Parameters=factor(Parameters))

Correlation coefficient between the observed data and the imputed ones.

ggplot(correlation) +
  geom_segment( aes(x=Parameters, xend=Parameters, y=Correlations_imputePCA, yend=Correlations_imputeMF), color="grey") +
  geom_point( aes(x=Parameters, y=Correlations_imputePCA), color=rgb(0.2,0.7,0.1,0.5), size=5 ) +
  geom_point( aes(x=Parameters, y=Correlations_imputeMF), color=rgb(0.7,0.2,0.1,0.5), size=5 ) +
  coord_flip()+
  theme_minimal() +
  xlab("") +
  ylab("Correlation coefficient")

Mean square error

MSE is predicting accuracy of a model. It is calculated as: MSE = (1/n) * Σ(actual – prediction)²
The lower the value for MSE, the more accurately a model is able to predict values.

correlation <- correlation %>% 
  rowwise() %>% 
  mutate( mymean = mean(c(MSE_imputePCA,MSE_imputeMF) )) %>% 
  arrange(mymean) %>% 
  mutate(Parameters=factor(Parameters))

Ecological parameters with the highest MSE value (removed F- estimation values to high), the worth imputed variables.

correlation %>% filter(mymean > 50 & mymean < 500) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_segment( aes(x=Parameters, xend=Parameters, y=MSE_imputePCA, yend=MSE_imputeMF), color="grey") +
  geom_point( aes(x=Parameters, y=MSE_imputePCA), color=rgb(0.2,0.7,0.1,0.5), size=5 ) +
  geom_point( aes(x=Parameters, y=MSE_imputeMF), color=rgb(0.7,0.2,0.1,0.5), size=5 ) +
  coord_flip()+
  theme_minimal() +
  xlab("") +
  ylab("MSE coefficient")

F test

F-test is used to assess whether the variances of two populations (A and B) are equal, here the observed data and the imputed ones.
H0: σ1² = σ2² (the population variances are equal).
/! sensitive to the normality of the sample distribution.

correlation <- correlation %>% 
  rowwise() %>% 
  mutate( mymean = mean(c(Ftest_imputePCA,Ftest_imputeMF) )) %>% 
  arrange(mymean) %>% 
  mutate(Parameters=factor(Parameters))

Ecological parameters with variance significantly different between the observed and imputed datas.

correlation  %>% filter(mymean < 0.3) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_segment( aes(x=Parameters, xend=Parameters, y=Ftest_imputePCA, yend=Ftest_imputeMF), color="grey") +
  geom_point( aes(x=Parameters, y=Ftest_imputePCA), color=rgb(0.2,0.7,0.1,0.5), size=5 ) +
  geom_point( aes(x=Parameters, y=Ftest_imputeMF), color=rgb(0.7,0.2,0.1,0.5), size=5 ) +
  coord_flip()+
  theme_minimal() +
  xlab("") +
  ylab("Ftest coefficient")

Chitest

Pearson’s Chi-squared Test is testing if there is a significant association between the categories of the two variables.
H0: the row and the column variables of the contingency table are independent.

Ecological parameters with a non significant p-value at Chi-sqaure test

comparison_obs_imputed %>% filter(Chitest_imputePCA >= 0.01) %>%
  arrange(Chitest_imputePCA) %>%    # First sort by val. This sort the dataframe but NOT the factor levels
  mutate(Parameters=factor(Parameters, levels=Parameters)) %>%   # This trick update the factor levels
  ggplot( aes(x=Parameters, y=Chitest_imputePCA)) +
  geom_segment( aes(xend=Parameters, yend=0)) +
  geom_point( size=4, color="orange") +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_bw() +
  xlab("")

2.Imputation of non random NA

Correlation

correlation <- comparison_obs_NorandomNA %>% 
  rowwise() %>% 
  mutate( mymean = mean(c(Correlations_imputePCA,Correlations_imputeMF) )) %>% 
  arrange(mymean) %>% 
  mutate(Parameters=factor(Parameters))

Correlation coefficient between the observed data and the imputed ones.

ggplot(correlation) +
  geom_segment( aes(x=Parameters, xend=Parameters, y=Correlations_imputePCA, yend=Correlations_imputeMF), color="grey") +
  geom_point( aes(x=Parameters, y=Correlations_imputePCA), color=rgb(0.2,0.7,0.1,0.5), size=5 ) +
  geom_point( aes(x=Parameters, y=Correlations_imputeMF), color=rgb(0.7,0.2,0.1,0.5), size=5 ) +
  coord_flip()+
  theme_minimal() +
  xlab("") +
  ylab("Correlation coefficient")

correlation %>% filter(mymean < 0.50) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_segment( aes(x=Parameters, xend=Parameters, y=Correlations_imputePCA, yend=Correlations_imputeMF), color="grey") +
  geom_point( aes(x=Parameters, y=Correlations_imputePCA), color=rgb(0.2,0.7,0.1,0.5), size=5 ) +
  geom_point( aes(x=Parameters, y=Correlations_imputeMF), color=rgb(0.7,0.2,0.1,0.5), size=5 ) +
  coord_flip()+
  theme_minimal() +
  xlab("") +
  ylab("Correlation coefficient")

Mean square error

MSE is predicting accuracy of a model. It is calculated as: MSE = (1/n) * Σ(actual – prediction)²
The lower the value for MSE, the more accurately a model is able to predict values.

correlation <- correlation %>% 
  rowwise() %>% 
  mutate( mymean = mean(c(MSE_imputePCA,MSE_imputeMF) )) %>% 
  arrange(mymean) %>% 
  mutate(Parameters=factor(Parameters))

Ecological parameters with the highest MSE value (removed F- estimation values to high), the worth imputed variables.

correlation %>% filter(mymean > 50 & mymean < 500) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_segment( aes(x=Parameters, xend=Parameters, y=MSE_imputePCA, yend=MSE_imputeMF), color="grey") +
  geom_point( aes(x=Parameters, y=MSE_imputePCA), color=rgb(0.2,0.7,0.1,0.5), size=5 ) +
  geom_point( aes(x=Parameters, y=MSE_imputeMF), color=rgb(0.7,0.2,0.1,0.5), size=5 ) +
  coord_flip()+
  theme_minimal() +
  xlab("") +
  ylab("MSE coefficient")

#ggsave("Higher MSE coefficient nonrandomNA.svg", plot =  last_plot(),dpi = 300, bg = "white", scale = 2 )

F test

F-test is used to assess whether the variances of two populations (A and B) are equal, here the observed data and the imputed ones.
H0: σ1² = σ2² (the population variances are equal).
/! sensitive to the normality of the sample distribution.

correlation <- correlation %>% 
  rowwise() %>% 
  mutate( mymean = mean(c(Ftest_imputePCA,Ftest_imputeMF) )) %>% 
  arrange(mymean) %>% 
  mutate(Parameters=factor(Parameters))

Ecological parameters with variance significantly different between the observed and imputed datas.

correlation  %>% filter(mymean < 0.05) %>%
  ggplot() +
  geom_segment( aes(x=Parameters, xend=Parameters, y=Ftest_imputePCA, yend=Ftest_imputeMF), color="grey") +
  geom_point( aes(x=Parameters, y=Ftest_imputePCA), color=rgb(0.2,0.7,0.1,0.5), size=5 ) +
  geom_point( aes(x=Parameters, y=Ftest_imputeMF), color=rgb(0.7,0.2,0.1,0.5), size=5 ) +
  coord_flip()+
  theme_minimal() +
  xlab("") +
  ylab("Ftest coefficient")

#ggsave("significant Ftest coefficient nonrandomNA.svg", plot =  last_plot(),dpi = 300, bg = "white", scale = 2 )

Chitest

Pearson’s Chi-squared Test is testing if there is a significant association between the categories of the two variables.
H0: the row and the column variables of the contingency table are independent.

Ecological parameters with a non significant p-value at Chi-sqaure test

comparison_obs_NorandomNA   %>% filter(Chitest_imputePCA >= 0.05)  %>%
  arrange(Chitest_imputePCA) %>%    # First sort by val. This sort the dataframe but NOT the factor levels
  mutate(Parameters=factor(Parameters, levels=Parameters)) %>%   # This trick update the factor levels
  ggplot( aes(x=Parameters, y=Chitest_imputePCA)) +
  geom_segment( aes(xend=Parameters, yend=0)) +
  geom_point( size=4, color="orange") +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_bw() +
  xlab("")

#ggsave("significant Chitest pvalue nonrandomNA.svg", plot =  last_plot(),dpi = 300, bg = "white", scale = 2 )
correlation <- correlation %>% 
  rowwise() %>% 
  mutate( mymean = mean(c(Chitest_imputePCA,Chitest_imputeMF) )) %>% 
  arrange(mymean) %>% 
  mutate(Parameters=factor(Parameters))

3. Distribution of non significant parameters

Selection of the ecological parameters with a non significant Chi test.

parameters_nosig <- comparison_obs_NorandomNA %>% filter(Chitest_imputePCA >0.01 )

Keeping interesting parameters (removing Ellenberg)

parameters_nosig <- parameters_nosig[1:22,]

Creating a data set with both imputed value and the observed data

data_tmp1 <- data_noNA %>% select(Type, all_of(parameters_nosig$Parameters))
data_tmp2 <- data_NorandomNA_impute_PCA %>% select(Type, all_of(parameters_nosig$Parameters))
colnames(data_tmp2)[-1] <- paste0(colnames(data_tmp2)[-1], "_PCA")
data_tmp3 <- data_NorandomNA_impute_MF %>% select(Type, all_of(parameters_nosig$Parameters))
colnames(data_tmp3)[-1] <- paste0(colnames(data_tmp3)[-1], "_MF")
data_impute_nosig <- cbind(data_tmp1, data_tmp2[,2:23], data_tmp3[,2:23])
data_impute_nosig_melt <- reshape2::melt(data_impute_nosig)
colnames(data_impute_nosig_melt) <- c("Type", "Parameters", "Value")
data_impute_nosig_melt$Method <- c(rep("aObserved", 2156), rep("PCA", 2156),rep("MF", 2156))
data_impute_nosig_melt$Parameters <-str_remove_all(data_impute_nosig_melt$Parameters, "_PCA")
data_impute_nosig_melt$Parameters <-str_remove_all(data_impute_nosig_melt$Parameters, "_MF")

Distribution of ecological parameters, observed data and the imputed ones based on the method of imputation.

data_impute_nosig_melt  %>% dplyr::filter(Parameters != "F_estimate") %>%
  ggplot( aes(x = Parameters ,y=Value, fill = Method)) + geom_boxplot() +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(data_impute_nosig_melt, aes(x = Parameters ,y= log(Value), fill = Method)) + geom_boxplot() +
  theme_minimal() + scale_fill_manual(values = c("#00C5CD", "#CD5555", "#32CD32"))

Linear model to test which method impute the best the data

data_impute_nosig_melt$Method <- factor(data_impute_nosig_melt$Method)
model <- lm(Value ~ Method/Parameters, data = data_impute_nosig_melt)
summary(model)
## 
## Call:
## lm(formula = Value ~ Method/Parameters, data = data_impute_nosig_melt)
## 
## Residuals:
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -115584     -27       0      12  317636 
## 
## Coefficients:
##                                            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
## (Intercept)                                  3.9080  1367.7205   0.003    0.998
## MethodMF                                    -0.1432  1934.2489   0.000    1.000
## MethodPCA                                   -0.1984  1934.2489   0.000    1.000
## MethodaObserved:ParametersCl_estimate      553.8148  1934.2489   0.286    0.775
## MethodMF:ParametersCl_estimate             466.6262  1934.2489   0.241    0.809

car::Anova(model)
## Anova Table (Type II tests)

## Response: Value
##                       Sum Sq   Df F value Pr(>F)    
## Method            2.2021e+08    2  0.6006 0.5485

## Residuals         1.1736e+12 6402                   
## ---
## Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Pairwise comparison

model <- lmer(Value ~ Parameters +Method + (Method|Parameters), data = data_impute_nosig_melt)
summary(model)
## Linear mixed model fit by REML. t-tests use Satterthwaite's method [
## lmerModLmerTest]
## Formula: Value ~ Parameters + Method + (Method | Parameters)
##    Data: data_impute_nosig_melt
## 
## REML criterion at convergence: 141017
## 
## Scaled residuals: 
##     Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
## -8.3056 -0.0111 -0.0054  0.0160 23.1660 
## 
## Random effects:
##  Groups     Name        Variance  Std.Dev. Corr       
##  Parameters (Intercept) 191686369 13845.1             
##             MethodMF       278009   527.3   0.16      
##             MethodPCA       39485   198.7  -0.16 -1.00
##  Residual               182727224 13517.7             
## Number of obs: 6468, groups:  Parameters, 22
## 
## Fixed effects:
##                            Estimate Std. Error         df t value Pr(>|t|)
## (Intercept)              -9.332e+01  1.386e+04  2.031e-07  -0.007    1.000
## ParametersCl_estimate     5.468e+02  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.028    1.000
## ParametersDW_105          2.364e+02  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.012    1.000
## ParametersDW_105_1/3      1.112e+01  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.001    1.000
## ParametersDW_55           9.884e+01  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.005    1.000
## ParametersDW_55_1/3       1.158e+01  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.001    1.000
## ParametersEvenness_index -2.979e+00  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.000    1.000
## ParametersF_estimate      5.681e+04  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   2.898    1.000
## ParametersFsoil_Vol       2.874e+02  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.015    1.000
## ParametersFW              3.065e+02  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.016    1.000
## ParametersFW_2/3          1.289e+02  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.007    1.000
## ParametersNO3_estimate    8.326e+02  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.042    1.000
## ParametersPO4_estimate   -4.184e+01  1.960e+04  2.030e-07  -0.002    1.000
## ParametersShading         6.874e+02  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.035    1.000
## ParametersShannon_index  -2.192e+00  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.000    1.000
## ParametersSk_Vol          9.538e+00  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.000    1.000
## ParametersSk_Vol_perc     7.014e-01  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.000    1.000
## ParametersSkW             3.067e+01  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.002    1.000
## ParametersSO4_estimate    7.556e+01  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.004    1.000
## Parameterstot_DW_55       1.363e+02  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.007    1.000
## Parameterstot_FW          3.427e+02  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.017    1.000
## Parameterstot_FW_2/3      1.641e+02  1.960e+04  2.030e-07   0.008    1.000
## MethodMF                 -3.368e+02  4.268e+02  6.069e+01  -0.789    0.433
## MethodPCA                 9.262e+01  4.139e+02  7.372e+02   0.224    0.823
## optimizer (nloptwrap) convergence code: 0 (OK)
## boundary (singular) fit: see help('isSingular')
car::Anova(model)
## Analysis of Deviance Table (Type II Wald chisquare tests)
## 
## Response: Value
##              Chisq Df Pr(>Chisq)
## Parameters 15.9360 21     0.7732
## Method      1.0477  2     0.5922

Use emmeans to compare the Method for each Parameters

emm <- emmeans(model, ~ Method | Parameters, pbkrtest.limit = 9408)
pairwise_comparisons <- contrast(emm, method = "pairwise")
summary(pairwise_comparisons)
## Parameters = C_%:
##  contrast        estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  aObserved - MF     336.8 427 21   0.789  0.7136
##  aObserved - PCA    -92.6 414 21  -0.224  0.9728
##  MF - PCA          -429.4 440 21  -0.976  0.5994
## 
## Parameters = Cl_estimate:

##  MF - PCA          -429.4 440 21  -0.976  0.5994

##  contrast        estimate  SE df t.ratio p.value
##  aObserved - MF     336.8 427 21   0.789  0.7136
##  aObserved - PCA    -92.6 414 21  -0.224  0.9728
##  MF - PCA          -429.4 440 21  -0.976  0.5994
## 
## Degrees-of-freedom method: kenward-roger 
## P value adjustment: tukey method for comparing a family of 3 estimates

IV Imputing the data set

Both PCA imputation method and Random forest are imputing the method correctly, PCA under estimate the values of the parameters compare to MF method, except for the NO3_estimate Both method will be used to impute the missing data and the mean of both imputation will be use in the next analysis.

data_to_impute <- Ecological_parameters %>% filter(Sowing_1st =="09/2021" ) %>% filter(Sowing_2nd =="no")
data_to_impute <- data_to_impute %>% filter(Destroyed !="02/2022" ) %>% filter(Destroyed !="04/2023")

data_to_impute$Site_ID <- as.factor(data_to_impute$Site_ID)
data_to_impute$Type <- as.factor(data_to_impute$Type)
data_to_impute <- data_to_impute[,c(1,22:151)]
data_to_impute <- as.data.frame(data_to_impute)
data_to_impute <- data_to_impute %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.character), as.numeric))  # Convert character columns to numeric if needed

dim(data_to_impute)
## [1] 368 131

1. PCA imputation

data_PCA_impute <- missMDA::imputePCA(data_to_impute, quanti.sup=130,quali.sup=1, ncp=2)$completeObs

2. MF imputation

data_MF_impute <- missForest(data_to_impute[,2:131])$ximp #remove factor column
data_MF_impute <- cbind("Site_ID"=data_to_impute$Site_ID, data_MF_impute)

3. Mean of the imputations

Ensure both data frames have the same dimensions

if (all(dim(data_PCA_impute) == dim(data_MF_impute))) {
  # Compute the mean
  data_imputed <- (data_PCA_impute[,2:131] + data_MF_impute[,2:131]) / 2
} else {
  stop("Data frames must have the same dimensions")
}

Add the phenology again to the data set

data_to_impute <- Ecological_parameters %>% filter(Sowing_1st =="09/2021" ) %>% filter(Sowing_2nd =="no")
data_to_impute <- data_to_impute %>% filter(Destroyed !="02/2022" ) %>% filter(Destroyed !="04/2023")

data_imputed <-cbind(data_to_impute[,1:21],data_imputed)

J. Josse, F. Husson et J. Pagès (2009) Gestion des données manquantes en Analyse en Composantes Principales. Journal de la SFdS. 150 (2), pp. 28-51. Josse, J., Husson, F. (2010). Multiple Imputation in PCA.

Stekhoven, D. J., & Bühlmann, P. (2011). MissForest—non-parametric missing value imputation for mixed-type data. Bioinformatics, 28(1), 112-118.

Petrazzini, B.O., Naya, H., Lopez-Bello, F. et al. Evaluation of different approaches for missing data imputation on features associated to genomic data. BioData Mining 14, 44 (2021). https://doi.org/10.1186/s13040-021-00274-7
